TL;DR: I copied this description from the `requests` library, which we delegate to; they default to `None` on the API, but then in the depths of the code they set `verify` to the *actual* default of `True` if not already set.

I'm inclined to stick with this description - and code setting! as-is, but happy to discuss further if you like?

References:
-  Description copied from: https://github.com/requests/requests/blob/master/requests/api.py#L39-L41 (or https://github.com/requests/requests/blob/master/requests/sessions.py#L474-L476, it's the same).
-  API defaulting to `None` in the API: https://github.com/requests/requests/blob/master/requests/sessions.py#L444.
-  Where they turn a `None` into `True`: https://github.com/requests/requests/blob/master/requests/sessions.py#L597 (with reference to https://github.com/requests/requests/blob/master/requests/sessions.py#L367-L368).

I don’t think this is the same thing as the original one. Here’s my thinking:

There are two types of degenerate faces:
- Faces with co-located vertices (an edge less than `tol.merge` in length)
- Faces with unique vertices, but the vertices are collinear

The first case is taken care of by `merge_vertices`, so any triangle passed to this function is guaranteed to have three edges with length at least `tol.merge`.

The second case should be addressed by this function. In the original implementation, the check was dependent on the area of the triangle, but when checking for collinearity, we probably don’t want to give different results if the mesh is simply scaled up. In the new implementation, I explicitly unitize the vectors to factor out scale, and then threshold the angle between them (the dot product). I think that this is probably cleaner, as it creates a clearer criterion for rejecting faces. What do you think?

An example of where the new function would differ from the old is with an equilateral triangle with side length `1.01e-8`. It seems that this should be allowed, but the old method would reject it, as the height of this triangle is less than `1e-8`. However, the new method would note that the angles are fine (60 degrees) and not flag the triangle as bad.

Actually, I probably don’t even need the validity check for `unitize` here — all of the edges must be greater than `1e-8` in length, since they were already fixed up by merging vertices. I’m thinking it can’t hurt to keep it, though :)

Let me know what you think! Sorry this is turning into a long PR comment chain haha, these sort of thresholding things tend to be messy. Definitely don’t change anything just for my use case!!! Only accept them if you think they make good sense in the general case.

Best,
Matt

Yes, I completely agree, the thing we need to determine is the behavior of `has_option`. Should `has_option` return True for `'something'` if `'something_cmd'` is defined?

If yes, then there will be no way for people to check if `'something_cmd'` is defined but `'something'` is not.

If `has_option` returns true for values defined on `self` and on `self.defaults` the only way to check if there's a default but no explicit value will be to check: `self.get('X') == self.defaults.get('X')`.

If we leave `has_option` without overriding, we will get some false negatives but we will not get any false positives.

> TMP_DIR is owned by git:slug, but the contents inside the directory is owned by git:git.

I propose to cache regexps at module level, like [here](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/helpers.py#L452
`re` module has 512 slots for LRU cache of compiled regexps.
Thus the program may degrade performance if other code uses non-complied exceptions for solving own tasks. If the total regexps number exceeds 512 -- `re` will recompile them constantly.

i'll try to write some benchmark of how multiple syscalls behave. I'm pretty sure the transport is almost always set before the body is written, so it's not really buffered here.

The main optimisation in all this is sending the buffered data as soon as the transport is set. Right now, the buffered data waits for the next chunk, and then is joined with this new chunk and everything is sent together. That behaves very poorly with a large body because it means copying everything to avoid a second syscall. For very small buffers it probably makes way more sense to bundle everything and do only one syscall

`ChainMap` resolves from the beginning of its `maps`. The point here is that `add_variable()` is called *after* resolving the subapp's routes, which means that subapp's match variables are added to the chain map first. Since we want to give priority to the innermost subapp's variables in the merged view of this chain map, this `append` is natural.

I thought a bit more about this...

The metadata we need here isn't in the package.json.

* The primary metadata we is the *location* in the Python package of the
JavaScript code. People could put npm packages anywhere in their python
package.
* Because of the typical way that people are using npm with Python
packages, they are often putting the pakage.json at the top-level of the
repo and then using webpack/browserify to copy the final build into the
Python package. Because of this, the package.json often isn't even in the
installed python package.

An example that shows both of these things is here:

https://github.com/jdfreder/jupyter-lightsaber

Summary: while I am 100% on board with us moving to npm, I don't think we
should go that way for the 4.2 nbextensions.

Cheers,

Brian

> I chimed in on this in the meeting, but perhaps starting to adopt the npm
> opinion now would be in order. Let's please not keep making new canonical
> locations and structures for the same string.
>
> Adopting this approach, once little extensions grow up and leave the nest,
> they already have all of their metadata ready to go, and could in turn be
> used by other kernels.
>
> Instead of _jupyter_nbextension_paths, perhaps
> _jupyter_nbextension_packages, where each of the paths returned must
> contain a package.json. In each of these, go ahead and claim the jupyter
> namespace, and have the dictionary that is currently returned by the python
> magic name instead be stored here.
>
> Here is an extreme way to encode all that information:
>
> # __init__.pydef _jupyter_nbextension_paths():
>     # yeah, cuz windows
>     return [
>         ["static", "some-extension"]
>     ]
>
> # package.json
> {
>   "name": "some-extension", # this is the install destination name
>   "version": "0.1.0",
>   "jupyter": {
>     "4": { # just be ready
>       "nbextension": {
>         "section": "notebook",
>         "src": ["."], # path bits, relative to this file
>         "require": "dist/index",
>       }
>     }
>   }
>   # ... all the other package stuff
> }
>
> Granted, this would be a bit weird for those of us that:
>
>    - have "mixed" repos that have package.json and setup.py at the same
>    level
>       - *stripping out package.json is a pretty gnarly anti-pattern as we
>       are starting to think about versioning*
>    -
>
>    use one package.json to configure multiple extensions
>    - *this is not the node way...*
>       - *but there's nothing stopping you from keeping this pattern, and
>       just having/building extra package.jsons*
>
>    Starting to reduce the amount of redundancy and freedom of choice (to
>    fail) in configuration would probably be worth it to me.
>
> —

> <https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/pull/879/files#r51464130>.

You don't need `additional_headers` assignment. You can get away with doing it in a single line:

`headers.update(kwargs.pop('headers', dict()))`

The way you have it right now, if `headers` isn't a key in `kwargs`, the default value of `dict()` will be returned. Then in the `headers.update` call, the empty dict will evaluate to be Falsy, which will incur another needless `dict()` creation. :) There is a design pattern called "factory", it allows you to create similar objects while keeping the specifics of the objects inside them. For example, now the ParameterScaler can be a divider, an amplifier, and also knows how to choose its role. The class becomes too big, complex, with repetitive code (the `if role == ..` stuff), not easily testable as you have to remember about all the possible things the class can do. Instead, an more maintainable design is to have a Divider class, an Amplifier class, and a "factory" class that provides the interface for creating them.

You can read more [on the wiki](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Factory_method_pattern) or take a look at [a somewhat close example in Python](https://krzysztofzuraw.com/blog/2016/factory-pattern-python.html).

So my proposal would be to:
* define Divider class (perhaps private so that it cannot be directly instantiated) that only divides
* define Amplifier class (perhaps private so that it cannot be directly instantiated) that only amplifies
* keep the ParameterScaler class as an "interface" that users will use to create Dividers or Amplifiers based on the key-value argument (`gain=` or `division=`)
* refactor existing tests so that they correspond to these classes.

Since it's based on module attribute lookup, using this inside `trio._core._result` means that we'll issue warnings when someone mentions `trio._core._result.<whatever>`, which isn't really what we want – it's references to `trio.hazmat.<whatever>` that people might doing and we want to deprecate. So we want to apply the `__deprecated_attributes__` stuff to `trio.hazmat`. In the version we used to have here we did that in `trio/__init__.py`, which seems as good as anything: https://github.com/python-trio/trio/commit/089c6ff02e624190d4cd3c4f3f2543cabcddfdec#diff-9bcb0dac3a23179589f66a8646fa38faL107

Also, this isn't really relevant because of the previous issue, but as an FYI: I think this also wouldn't work because the `__deprecated_attributes__` stuff is only checked if there isn't a real variable of the same name. (This is an optimization to avoid slowdowns on accessing non-deprecated attributes; it works using `__getattr__`.) So here where are actual variables named `Value`, `Error`, `Result` in the same module, it makes `__deprecated_attributes__` a no-op.
